REFERENCE: Multiple serious inconsistencies render this citation inauthentic. (1) The title references COVID-19, yet the date is given as 2002 — COVID-19 did not exist until late 2019/2020. (2) The DOI (10.1016/S0140-6736(21)02349-7) contains the ISSN S0140-6736, which belongs to The Lancet, not The New England Journal of Medicine (NEJM ISSN is 0028-4793). (3) The issue number 10315 is implausibly high for NEJM, which typically uses issue numbers in the low hundreds. (4) The volume (398) could correspond to NEJM around 2020, but combined with a 2002 date, it is nonsensical. (5) The page range and article ID format suggest the DOI may belong to a Lancet article, not an NEJM article. Taken together, this citation appears fabricated or heavily manipulated with mismatched components from different sources.
authors: The single listed author 'Nash, Z.B.' does not appear to be an established or well-known author in the field of mobile health or COVID-19. Without corroborating sources linking this author to the stated title, this component cannot be verified.
title: The title 'Beyond COVID-19: scaling up and sustaining mobile health in Africa' is thematically plausible for recent global health literature (post-2020). However, no corroborating evidence links this exact title to the stated author, journal, or DOI. The title's plausibility alone does not validate the citation.
journal_name: The cited journal is 'The New England journal of medicine,' but the DOI and ISSN embedded in the DOI correspond to The Lancet. The journal name is mismatched with the DOI's bibliographic source.
journal_volume: Volume 398 for NEJM corresponds roughly to the year 2021, which is plausible for a COVID-19-related article. However, this conflicts with the stated year 2002 in the citation.
journal_issue: The issue number '10315' is extremely unusual and implausible for The New England Journal of Medicine, which uses relatively small issue numbers (typically in the range of 1–30 per volume). This appears fabricated or erroneously inserted.
journal_page: Pages 1962-1963 for an NEJM volume 398 issue are plausible in format but cannot be verified as belonging to NEJM given the DOI points to a different journal.
elocator: The article e455286 is presented as an NEJM e-locator, but since the DOI points to The Lancet, this identifier is inconsistent. NEJM and The Lancet have different article identification systems.
publication_date: The date '2002 Feb 1' is irreconcilable with the title's reference to COVID-19, which only emerged in late 2019. This is a clear fabrication or error. The actual publication year, if this article existed, would likely be 2021 based on the volume and DOI date.
doi: The DOI '10.1016/S0140-6736(21)02349-7' uses the ISSN S0140-6736, which is the identifier for The Lancet, a completely different journal from The New England Journal of Medicine. The DOI prefix 10.1016 (Elsevier) is consistent with The Lancet, not NEJM (which uses 10.1056). The DOI, if valid, would lead to a Lancet article, not the NEJM article cited here.
pmcid: No PMCID is included in this citation, so this component is absent. Its absence neither confirms nor denies authenticity.
pmid: No PMID is included in this citation, so this component is absent. Its absence neither confirms nor denies authenticity.
